metadata.title: Claude, Debunked?
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity UI beats; the WHEEL keeps the original infographic's palette on purpose — it is the exhibit being graded, not a brand surface.
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Samoan. Wagwan check: charsum('claude-debunked') % 10 == 1, not 0.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI at B00 (with RESULT lines) / verdict / handoff / outro. B01-B04 + B06 = the EcosystemWheel exhibit (grade/startAt/focus props, scaled onto the 16:9 stage) — legal because the wheel IS the subject under audit; B05 PredictCard. Rebuilds the partial 1000x1200 ecosystem-wheel-graded.mp4 as a full 16:9 episode. Evidence: FACTCHECK-THE-FACTCHECK.md (verified 2026-07-14, primary sources cited per claim).

beats.narration_text: The twist that the debunker committed the mirror-image sin, un-inventing real products like Claude Marketplace and Claude Security, is the failure mode I want to guard against. I'll take a suspiciously tidy list of claims. Can you help me (1) separate which entries I can verify as real versus stale versus invented, (2) name the exact evidence a real verification would require instead of 'sounds like template filler', and (3) show how pattern-confidence could push me toward both over-inventing and over-debunking the very same list?

props.command: The twist that the debunker committed the mirror-image sin, un-inventing real products like Claude Marketplace and Claude Security, is the failure mode I want to guard against. I'll take a suspiciously tidy list of claims. Can you help me (1) separate which entries I can verify as real versus stale versus invented, (2) name the exact evidence a real verification would require instead of 'sounds like template filler', and (3) show how pattern-confidence could push me toward both over-inventing and over-debunking the very same list?
output: the wheel: real core, invented filler
the viral fact-check: right — and wrong twice
the flagship is missing from both

beats.narration: There's a wheel going around that maps the entire Claude ecosystem — and a viral fact-check tearing it apart. Here's the fun part — this is Liam, in for Bear — they're both wrong, in mirror image. Today we grade the wheel AND the graders.

beats.narration_text: The wheel first, rebuilt box by box. Eight categories, symmetric spokes, a tidy grid of products — and that symmetry is your first tell. Real product families are never this neat. But tidy doesn't mean fake. Let's actually check.

The core holds up. Opus, Haiku, the Agent S D K, Managed Agents, Claude Code, Cowork, M C P — real and shipping. Claude Design too — a real Anthropic Labs preview, though it runs Opus four point seven, not four point eight like the wheel says.

The stale ring. Sonnet four point six on the wheel — it's Sonnet five now. Mythos still invite-only, no public rollout announced. And the miss almost nobody caught: Fable five isn't on the wheel at all. The flagship — missing from the map of its own ecosystem.

Now the invented ring — thirteen boxes. Claude Context Store. Safe Artifact Repo. A Constitutional A I checker you can apparently buy. None of these exist as products. They're features and training methods wearing product costumes, invented to complete the pattern. The fact-check called all of this correctly.
So the fact-check wins? Not yet. If the wheel's sin is inventing products to complete a pattern — predict the mirror-image sin a debunker commits. Commit to it.

props.question: What's the debunker's mirror-image mistake?
props.commit: commit — the twist is one beat away

beats.narration_text: It un-invents them. Claude Marketplace — filed as fake — is a real product; enterprises buy partner tools there today. Claude Security — also filed as fake — real, shipping. 'Sounds like template filler' is not a source. Both sides failed exactly the same way: pattern-confidence outrunning verification.

Let's recap with Claude. The verdict, both directions. The wheel: real core, stale models, thirteen invented boxes, and the flagship missing entirely. The fact-check: right about the filler, wrong about two real products. One rule falls out of this episode: a fact-check that doesn't cite sources is just a second opinion.

artifactLines: The wheel: real core (models · SDK · Code · Cowork · MCP · Design) — stale models ring, 13 invented boxes.
Missing from the wheel entirely: Claude Fable 5 — the flagship.
The fact-check: right on the filler, wrong twice — Marketplace and Security are real, shipping products.
The rule: a fact-check that doesn't cite sources is just a second opinion.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — run this on the next viral diagram in your feed, and hold one line: no source, no verdict. Claude will show receipts, box by box.

props.command: Here's an infographic I want checked. For every box: real product, real feature dressed as a product, or invented? Cite a primary source for each verdict — no source, no verdict.

beats.narration: Claude, debunked — question mark. If this saved you a reshare, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
